coredata.dc:title: Designing of multifunctional graphene quantum dot-polyvinyl alcohol-polyglycerol luminescent film for fluorescence detection of pH in sweat
coredata.dc:creator: Qingqing, Xie
Ruiyi, Li
Zaijun, Li
coredata.dc:description:
Background Wound infection, skin disease, renal failure, cancer, cystic fibrosis, and other pathologies may induce obvious pH changes in sweat. Thus, tracking skin pH changes can help monitor human health in a convenient manner. Owing to their biocompatibility, easy preparation, and sensitive response to pH changes, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have received increased attention in the optical detection of pH changes. However, their poor luminescent efficiency under visible light excitation and lack of functional diversification limit their application in skin pH monitoring. Therefore, the development of GQDs with excellent ultraviolet protection ability and antibacterial and luminescence performance is essential.
Results Folic acid-, histidine-, and serine-functionalized boron-doped graphene quantum dots (FHSB-GQDs) were designed and synthesized via thermal treatment. The resulting FHSB-GQDs exhibit strong yellow fluorescence emission under excitation with 490-nm visible light and sensitive pH responsiveness. The peak fluorescence intensity linearly decreases with increasing pH from 4 to 9. Furthermore, the FHSB-GQDs were integrated with polyvinyl alcohol and polyglycerol to form a luminescent film via hydrogen bond interactions. The film exhibits high transparency, mechanical flexibility, ultraviolet protection ability, and antibacterial activity. The presence of polyvinyl alcohol and polyglycerol restricts the free movement of the FHSB-GQDs and improves fluorescence behavior. The film was successfully applied in an intelligent pH-sensing system for monitoring pH changes in human sweat.
Significance The graphene quantum dot–polyvinyl alcohol–polyglycerol luminescent film offers excellent transparency, mechanical flexibility, ultraviolet protection ability, antibacterial activity, and luminescence performance. It was successfully applied in an intelligent pH sensing system for the detection of pH changes in human sweat. This study provides a new strategy for the design and construction of wearable sensing systems for health monitoring, facial masks, and medical dressings.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dot
Ultraviolet protection
Antibacterial activity
Fluorescence probe
pH analysis

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Folic acid, histidine and serine-functionalized and boron-doped graphene quantum dot (FHSB-GQD) synthesis
Construction principle, mechanical property, ultraviolet protection ability, and antibacterial activity of the FHSB-GQD–PVA–PG film
Fluorescence behavior of the FHSB-GQD–PVA–PG film
Application in detection of skin pH

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Key Research and Development Program of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Key Research and Development Program of China
National Key Research and Development Program of China Stem Cell and Translational Research

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: School of Chemical and Material Engineering
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: School of Chemical Engineering and Advanced Materials, University of Adelaide

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: Folic acid, citric acid, histidine, serine, H3BO3, NaH2PO4·12H2O, Na2HPO4·2H2O, NaCl, and other reagents employed were all of the highest analytical grade or quality reagents purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagents Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) with an average polymerization degree of 1700 ± 50 and alcoholysis degree of 99 %, was supported by SINOPEC Sichuan Vinylon Works, China. The dialysis tubing (MWCO 3000 Da) was purchased from Yuanyebio Co., Ltd, China. Britton-Robinson buffer was prepared by mixing the equal volume of 0.04 mol L−1 phosphoric acid, boric acid and acetic acid and then adjusting to the required pH with sodium hydroxide solution. All materials were used without further purification. The ultrapure water (18.2 MΩ cm) purified from a Milli-Q purification system was used throughout the experiment.The authors acknowledge the financial support from The National Key Research and Development Program of China (No.2021YFA0910200) and National Natural Science Foundation of China (No.21576115). We also would like to acknowledge the work of the Central Laboratory, School of Chemical and Material Engineering, Jiangnan University.

The authors acknowledge the financial support from The National Key Research and Development Program of China (No.2021YFA0910200) and National Natural Science Foundation of China (No.21576115). We also would like to acknowledge the work of the Central Laboratory, School of Chemical and Material Engineering, Jiangnan University.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM image (A), HRTEM image (B), AFM image (C), thickness distribution curve (D), absorption spectrum (E) and FTIR spectrum (F) of FHSB-GQD.
Total XPS spectrum (A) and high resolution C1s (B), N1s and B1s XPS spectra of FHSB-GQD.

Three-dimensional fluorescence mapping of FHSB-GQD aqueous solution (A), fluorescence spectra of FHSB-GQD aqueous solution at the excitation of different wavelength lights (B), plot of the excitation wavelength vs. the maximum fluorescence emission wavelength (C), plot of peak fluorescence intensity vs. the wavelength of excitation light (D), fluorescence emission spectra and fluorescence excitation spectrum at the excitation wavelength of 490 nm visible light, and the peak fluorescence intensities at 550 nm at the excitation of 490 nm visible light for the different irradiation times with an ultraviolet lamp of 100 W of FHSB-GQD. Other conditions: the FHSB-GQD concentration: 0.05 mg mL−1, the medium acidity: the PBS of pH 7.4, the temperature: 22 °C.

Fluorescence spectra of FHSB-GQD under the excitation of 490 nm visible light in different pH (A), plot of peak fluorescence intensity vs. pH (B), peak fluorescence intensities at pH 4 and pH 9 for different cycle times (C), and change (F/F0) in the peak fluorescence intensities caused by interfere species (D).

Construction principle of FHSB-GQD-PVA-PG film (A), plots of the transmittance vs. the wavelength for FHSB-GQD-PVA-PG and PVA-PG films (B), optical photographs of FHSB-GQD-PVA-PG films in different deformation states with irradiation of laser pointer of 490 nm (C), the stress-strain curve of FHSB-GQD-PVA-PG film (D), ultraviolet–visible absorption spectrum of FHSB-GQD-PVA-PG (E), optical photos of PVA-PG film incubated Escherichia coli (F) and Staphylococcus aureus for 48 h (G), and optical photos of FHSB-GQD-PVA-PG film incubated Escherichia coli (H) and Staphylococcus aureus for 48 h (I).

The three-dimensional fluorescence mapping of FHSB-GQD-PVA-PG film (A), fluorescence spectra of FHSB-GQD-PVA-PG film under the excitation of 320, 330, 340, 350, 360, 370, 380, 390, 400, 410, 420, 430, 440, 450, 460, 470, 480, 490, 500, 510 and 520 nm (B), CIE coordinate excited within a wide wavenumber range of 320–520 nm (C), optical photo of FHSB-GQD-PVA-PG film under 490 nm laser irradiation (D), fluorescence spectra of FHSB-GQD-PVA-PG film at different pH stimulations, and plot of the peak fluorescence intensity vs. pH (F).

Construction principle of pH-detector with FHSB-GQD-PVA-PG film (A), fluorescence spectra of FHSB-GQD-PVA-PG film at different exercise time (B) and wearable intelligent pH sensing system with FHSB-GQD-PVA-PG film (C).

head.ce:title: Designing of multifunctional graphene quantum dot-polyvinyl alcohol-polyglycerol luminescent film for fluorescence detection of pH in sweat

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Synthetic and Biological Colloids, Ministry of Education, School of Chemical and Material Engineering, School of Life Science and Health Engineering, Jiangnan University, Wuxi, 214122, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Synthetic and Biological Colloids
School of Chemical and Material Engineering
School of Life Science and Health Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Synthetic and Biological Colloids, Ministry of Education, School of Chemical and Material Engineering, School of Life Science and Health Engineering, Jiangnan University, Wuxi, 214122, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Wound infection, skin disease, renal failure, cancer, cystic fibrosis, and other pathologies may induce obvious pH changes in sweat. Thus, tracking skin pH changes can help monitor human health in a convenient manner. Owing to their biocompatibility, easy preparation, and sensitive response to pH changes, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have received increased attention in the optical detection of pH changes. However, their poor luminescent efficiency under visible light excitation and lack of functional diversification limit their application in skin pH monitoring. Therefore, the development of GQDs with excellent ultraviolet protection ability and antibacterial and luminescence performance is essential.

Folic acid-, histidine-, and serine-functionalized boron-doped graphene quantum dots (FHSB-GQDs) were designed and synthesized via thermal treatment. The resulting FHSB-GQDs exhibit strong yellow fluorescence emission under excitation with 490-nm visible light and sensitive pH responsiveness. The peak fluorescence intensity linearly decreases with increasing pH from 4 to 9. Furthermore, the FHSB-GQDs were integrated with polyvinyl alcohol and polyglycerol to form a luminescent film via hydrogen bond interactions. The film exhibits high transparency, mechanical flexibility, ultraviolet protection ability, and antibacterial activity. The presence of polyvinyl alcohol and polyglycerol restricts the free movement of the FHSB-GQDs and improves fluorescence behavior. The film was successfully applied in an intelligent pH-sensing system for monitoring pH changes in human sweat.

The graphene quantum dot–polyvinyl alcohol–polyglycerol luminescent film offers excellent transparency, mechanical flexibility, ultraviolet protection ability, antibacterial activity, and luminescence performance. It was successfully applied in an intelligent pH sensing system for the detection of pH changes in human sweat. This study provides a new strategy for the design and construction of wearable sensing systems for health monitoring, facial masks, and medical dressings.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: The pH is an important biomarker to monitor human health status. Abnormal fluctuation of pH may indicate the occurrence and development of various diseases [1]. For example, pH alterations in extracellular fluids have been associated with cancer [2], Alzheimer's disease [3], chronic obstructive pulmonary disease [4], and wound healing [5]. The fluctuation in intracellular pH may alter cellular actions such as the adaptation of cellular volume, membrane polarity, and signaling [6]. The functional disorder of cells is often related to abnormal pH changes because the structural stability of proteins depends on pH. Low pH values may cause protein denaturation and enzyme activation [7]. Recently, pH was applied as a critical biomarker in human skin. Normal life processes can only produce minimal fluctuations in skin pH in the range of 5.4–5.9. However, wound infection, skin diseases, renal failure, cancer, cystic fibrosis, and other pathologies can induce changes in skin pH to slightly alkaline values of 7–9 [8]. Therefore, the detection of skin pH is important to monitor human health status.

Currently, the main available analytical methods for the detection of pH are electrochemical [9], nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) spectroscopy [10], colorimetric [11], and fluorescence methods [12]. The electrochemical methods offer high sensitivity, rapidity, and cost effectiveness but suffer from interference from electroactive species in sweat. The NMR spectroscopy is a convenient noncontact analysis method; however, it requires expensive instruments and skilled operators, which limit its wide application in routine pH analysis. The colorimetric methods are simple and selective, but their sensitivity is lower than that of the electrochemical and fluorescence methods. Meanwhile, the fluorescence methods based on luminescent materials have received increasing attention for the detection of pH in body fluids, cells, and tissues because of their high sensitivity, convenience, and noninvasive features. The available luminescent materials for the detection of pH include transition-metal semiconductors such as CdTe quantum dots (CdTe QDs) [13], precious metal atomic clusters such as Au nanoclusters (Au NCs) [14], Ti2C3 quantum dots (Ti2C3 QDs) [15], and graphene quantum dots (GQDs) [16]. The CdTe QDs show high luminescent efficiency and adjustable fluorescence performance but may introduce highly toxic Cd2+. The Au NCs achieve high luminescence efficiency but are expensive, and the scarcity of resources limits their application. The Ti2C3 QDs exhibit biocompatibility; however, they are prone to oxidation in air, which substantially reduces their luminescence efficiency. The GQDs have recently emerged as a new type of carbon nanomaterial. Unlike classical graphene, GQDs consist of small functionalized graphene sheets with a size of several nanometers. Their unique structure endows GQDs with special photoelectric properties, rendering them applicable as an optical probe for bioanalysis [17], biomedicine [18], photoelectric conversion [19], and photocatalysis [20]. Unfortunately, the low luminescence efficiency of GQDs under visible light excitation and lack of functional diversification limit their application in skin pH monitoring.

Various methods have been adopted to improve the fluorescence behavior and functional diversification of GQDs. One approach is heteroatom doping into the carbon skeleton of the graphene sheets. The unique electronic structure of heteroatoms causes an obvious change in the electronic structure of the graphene sheets, which enables the effective regulation of the optical property. For instance, Zheng et al. reported the synthesis of N-doped GQDs via the hydrothermal treatment of oxalic acid and ethylenediamine [21], revealing that the introduction of N atoms increases the electron density of the graphene sheets and, in turn, the luminous efficacy. The resulting N-doped GQDs were applied in the optical detection of pH in living cells and hemoglobin in blood. In addition to nonmetallic elements, such as N [22], S [23], and B [24], metal elements have been used to construct metal-doped GQDs. Unlike nonmetallic elements, metal ions can combine with functional groups at the edge of the graphene sheets via coordination bonds [25], reducing the electron density of the graphene sheets and the fluorescence emission intensity. However, the doping metal may considerably alter the energy band structure, thereby changing the fluorescence emission. Mondal et al. reported the synthesis of Eu–Tb-co-doped GQDs via the coordination of GQDs with Tb3+ and Eu3+ [26]. The resulting Eu–Tb-GQDs produced eight fluorescence emission peaks at 490, 545, 594, 616, 621, 651, 685, and 695 nm and were successfully applied in the fluorescence detection of Cr(VI) and pH. Another approach to enhance the performance of GQDs involves introducing functional groups into the edge of the graphene sheets, which endows GQDs with special functions and further expands their applications. Hai et al. reported folic acid-functionalized GQDs (FA-GQDs) [27]. The introduction of folic acid enables the specific recognition of HeLa cells for bioimaging. The third approach involves the combination of GQDs with polymers to form GQD-based composite films. Owing to the integration of the good mechanical properties of polymers with the good luminescence behavior of GQDs, GQD-based composite films can be applied in the construction of wearable sensing systems. Laysandra et al. reported the synthesis of a self-healable photoluminescent film by mixing N-doped GQDs and a PBA0.8-co-PNMA0.2 copolymer [28]. They found that using N-doped GQDs as a nanocrosslinker improves the mechanical properties of the copolymer film. The dispersion of the copolymer on the N-doped GQDs and the interaction between the functional groups of the N-doped GQDs and copolymer enhance the stability and selectivity of the fluorescence response to pH. However, despite these advances, the fabrication of an ideal GQD-based film with high ultraviolet protection ability, antibacterial activity, and luminescence performance under visible light excitation remains a challenging task.

Herein, we report the design and synthesis of folic acid-, histidine-, and serine-functionalized B-doped GQD (FHSB-GQD) with good ultraviolet protection ability, antibacterial activity, fluorescence performance under excitation with 490-nm visible light, and pH responsiveness, which can be further improved by introducing polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) and polyglycerol (PG). The luminescent FHSB-GQD–PVA–PG film was successfully applied in the fluorescence detection of pH changes in human sweat.

Folic acid, citric acid, histidine, serine, H3BO3, NaH2PO4·12H2O, Na2HPO4·2H2O, NaCl, and other reagents employed were all of the highest analytical grade or quality reagents purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagents Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) with an average polymerization degree of 1700 ± 50 and alcoholysis degree of 99 %, was supported by SINOPEC Sichuan Vinylon Works, China. The dialysis tubing (MWCO 3000 Da) was purchased from Yuanyebio Co., Ltd, China. Britton-Robinson buffer was prepared by mixing the equal volume of 0.04 mol L−1 phosphoric acid, boric acid and acetic acid and then adjusting to the required pH with sodium hydroxide solution. All materials were used without further purification. The ultrapure water (18.2 MΩ cm) purified from a Milli-Q purification system was used throughout the experiment.

Fluorescence spectrum and intensity were measured on a Cary Esclipse fluorescence spectrophotometer (Agilent, Japan). Transmission electron microscope (TEM) images were conducted on JEOL 2010 FEG microscope at 200 keV. Atomic force microscopic (AFM) images were taken by Multimode 8 Force Microscope (Bruker, Germany). Infrared spectra (IR) were recorded by Nicolet FT-IR 6700 spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific Co., Ltd., America). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopic (XPS) measurements were performed on the ESCALAB 250Xi photoemission spectrometer having Al Kα (1486.6 eV) dual anode as the source operating at 14.6 kV of anode voltage and 13.5 mA filament current. The XPS data was collected with pass energy of 20 eV at 4xl0−9 m bar vacuum. Initially the XPS unit was calibrated using Fermi edge of Ag (KE 1482.544). The C 1s line of adventitious carbon with BE = 284.8 eV was taken as a reference for surface-charging corrections. After subtracting the linear-type background (or Shirley background for fitting the full Ta 4f regions), the core-level spectra were decomposed into their components with mixed Gaussian-Lorentzian lines by a non-linear least squares curve-fitting procedure, using the CasaXPS software. The binding energies and FWHM of the peaks were determined from the fitting results. The UV–visible diffuse reflectance spectra (DRS) was measured on Shimadzu UV-3600 Plus. UV–visible spectra were recorded on a TU-1901 spectrometer with DH-2000 deuterium and tungsten halogen light source in the absorbance mode (Beijing Purkinje General Instrument Co., Ltd., China).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Folic acid, histidine and serine-functionalized and boron-doped graphene quantum dot (FHSB-GQD) synthesis
ce:section.ce:para: The FHSB-GQD was prepared by thermal treatment of folic acid, histidine, serine and boric acid [29]. Typically, 3.0 g of citric acid was mixed with 0.063 g of folic acid, 2.214 g of histidine, 0.3 g of serine and H3BO3 (0.13 g) under stirring. The resulting mixture was transferred into a high-temperature oven and then heated at 180 °C for 3 h. The collected FHSB-GQD crude was dissolved in ultrapure water to produce 50 mg mL−1 of FHSB-GQD aqueous solution. To obtain solid FHSB-GQD product, it was treated by filtering to remove large graphene sheets, dialysis with 3000 Da to remove unreacted raw materials and freeze-drying. The procedure was also used for synthesis of graphene quantum dot (GQD), histidine-functionalized graphene quantum dot (H-GQD), histidine and serine-functionalized graphene quantum dot (HS-GQD), and folic acid, histidine and serine-functionalized graphene quantum dot (FHS-GQD).

The FHSB-GQD-PVA-PG film was prepared by dissolving 6 g of polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) and 23.3 g of polyglycerol (PG) in 15 mL of 4 mg mL−1 FHSB-GQD aqueous solution. The mixture was heated at 98 °C till a transparent and homogeneous solution was formed, cooled to room temperature and poured into a Watch glass. It was treated by freezing at −20 °C for 12 h and then unfreezing for 1h. To obtain FHSB-GQD-PVA-PG film, the above freeze-thaw process was cycled for 2 times.

The FHSB-GQD aqueous solution (1.0 mg mL−1, 0.5 mL) was added into 2 mL of centrifuge tube. After added 1.5 mL of the BR buffer with different pH, it was stirred and then subject to measure the fluorescence emission spectrum and fluorescence intensity on fluorescence photometer at the excitation of 490 nm or other wavelengths. During the measuring, the slit widths of the excitation and emission were set to 2 nm and the voltage was 700 V.
The fluorescence quantum yield measurement was shown in ESI.

Multifunctional FHSB-GQD was designed and synthesized to enhance the ultraviolet protection ability, antibacterial activity, luminescence performance under visible light excitation, and pH responsiveness of GQD (Fig. 1 ). Citric acid was used as a carbon source to fabricate the graphene sheets via intermolecular condensation and subsequent carbonization [29]. Folic acid, histidine, and serine were used as functional molecules to introduce special functional groups into the edge of the graphene sheets. Folic acid and histidine contain aromatic rings and carboxyl groups with a large electron-conjugated system. The π–π conjugation between the graphene sheets and folic acid and histidine groups redshifts the absorption spectrum of GQD, leading to an increased fluorescence intensity under visible light excitation. Serine contains electron-donating hydroxyl and amino groups whose introduction enhances the electron density in the graphene sheets via p–π and n–π conjugations. This improves the luminescence efficiency. Meanwhile, the introduction of electron-deficient B improves the ability of the graphene sheets to attract electron pairs from peripheral functional groups, thereby promoting electron transfer from the functional groups to the graphene sheets for enhanced luminescence efficiency. As a result, the synergy between these three factors alters the energy band structure of GQD and narrows the bandgap energy, thereby considerably enhancing the capacity of the FHSB-GQD to absorb visible light and leading to strong fluorescence emission under visible light excitation. In addition to improving the fluorescence behavior, the introduction of folic acid, histidine, and serine endows the FHSB-GQD with multifunction. The aromatic rings and graphene sheets of the FHSB-GQDs can strongly absorb ultraviolet light, conferring excellent ultraviolet protection ability to the FHSB-GQD. The hydrophilic groups of the FHSB-GQD can strongly interact with the hydrophilic groups on the surface of bacteria via van der Waals forces and hydrogen bonds, which limits the normal life activities of bacteria. Moreover, H+ in water can protonate the amino and imino groups in the FHSB-GQD. The positive electric field stemming from the protonation of the FHSB-GQDs seriously disrupts the normal life activity of bacteria. The combination of these two factors greatly restricts the growth and reproduction of bacteria, thereby endowing the FHSB-GQD with high antibacterial activity. The O- and N-containing groups of the FHSB-GQD undergo different degrees of dissociation and protonation in different pH environments. These protonation and dissociation processes cause changes in the fluorescence emission, providing the FHSB-GQD with sensitive pH responsiveness.

The as-designed FHSB-GQDs were synthesized via thermal treatment with citric acid, folic acid, histidine, serine, and boric acid at 180 °C for 3 h. To improve the fluorescence behavior, the effect of the histidine/citric, serine/citric, folic/citric, and boric/citric acid molar ratios on the fluorescence emission was studied, finding a dependence of the fluorescence behavior with the molar ratios. As shown in Figure s1, the optimal molar ratios are histidine/citric acid = 1.0, serine/citric acid = 0.2, boric acid/citric acid = 0.2, and folic acid/citric acid = 0.02.

The as-synthesized FHSB-GQD was characterized via transmission electron microscopy (TEM), high-resolution TEM (HRTEM), atomic force microscopy (AFM), ultraviolet–visible absorption spectroscopy, Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), and by plotting the transformed Kubelka–Munk function vs. the energy of light of the FHSB-GQDs. The TEM image shows several black spots (Fig. 2 A) corresponding to zero-dimensional graphene sheets with sizes ranging from 1 to 10 nm and an average size of 4.0 ± 0.35 nm (Figure s2). The HRTEM image exhibits a lattice spacing of 0.28 nm, which corresponds to the (002) crystal face of graphene (Fig. 2B). The AFM image displays numerous white dots (Fig. 2C), with a thickness ranging from 0.2 to 3.0 nm, indicating that the FHSB-GQDs are mainly composed of 1–11 layers of the graphene sheets (Fig. 2D). The absorption spectrum of the FHSB-GQDs includes three absorption peaks at 228, 336, and 467 nm (Fig. 2E). The peak at 228 nm can be attributed to ultraviolet absorption by the functional groups at the edges of the graphene sheets. The peaks at 336 and 467 nm can be ascribed to the absorption of ultraviolet–visible light by the graphene sheets. Because the graphene sheets exhibit a much larger π-electron delocalization range, the absorption peaks are obviously redshifted to the near-ultraviolet and visible light regions. Compared with classical GQD, the FHSB-GQD exhibit a much stronger absorption capacity to ultraviolet light, which can be attributed to the introduction of folic acid and histidine, further extending the π-electron delocalization range. The FTIR spectrum of the FHSB-GQDs contains seven absorption peaks and bands (Fig. 2F). A wide and strong absorption band owing to the symmetric stretching vibration of N–H and O–H bonds appears at about 3433 cm−1. The absorption peak at 3149 cm−1 is caused by the stretching vibration of saturated C–H bonds. The absorption peak at 1709 cm−1 is owing to the stretching vibration of the CO bond. The absorption peak at 1627 cm−1 corresponds to the stretching vibration of the skeleton CC bonds in the aromatic rings. The peaks at 1391 and 1093 cm−1 can be attributed to the stretching vibration of the C–H and C–O bonds. These results demonstrate the existence of –OH, –NH–, –NH2, and –COOH in the FHSB-GQD.

Fig. 3 shows the full XPS spectrum of the FHSB-GQDs and high-resolution C 1s, N 1s, and B 1s XPS spectra. The full XPS spectrum includes four peaks at 189.7, 284.6, 398.1, and 531 eV, corresponding to the B 1s, C 1s, N 1s, and O 1s core levels, respectively, demonstrating that the FHSB-GQDs are mainly composed of B, C, N, and O elements. The high-resolution C 1s XPS spectrum could be deconvoluted into six peaks at 284.1, 284.6, 285.6, 286.2, 287.4, and 288.3 eV, which can be assigned to C–B, C–C, CC, C–O, C–N, and –COOH bonds, respectively. The high-resolution N 1s XPS spectrum could be deconvoluted into four peaks at 395.2, 396.3, 398.1, and 398.4 eV, corresponding to amine N, pyridinic N, graphitic N, and pyrrolic N, respectively. The high-resolution B 1s XPS spectrum could be deconvoluted into three peaks at 188.7, 189.6, and 190.5 eV, corresponding to B–N, B–O, and B–C bonds, respectively. These results confirm that the doped B atoms are located on the carbon skeleton and on the edges of the graphene sheets.

Figure s3 shows the plot of the transformed Kubelka–Munk function vs. the energy of light for the GQDs and FHSB-GQDs. The bandgap energy values (Ep) of GQDs and the FHSB-GQDs were obtained using the intersection point of the tangent line of the plots and abscissa. The Ep value of GQDs reaches 3.23 eV. Owing to their wide bandgap, GQDs exhibit an absorption capacity toward ultraviolet light and an extremely weak absorption capacity toward visible light. Therefore, GQDs can only produce strong fluorescence emission under ultraviolet light excitation. With increasing excitation wavelength, the fluorescence intensity rapidly declines. By contrast, the FHSB-GQDs show a relatively low Ep value of 1.31 eV, which results in a strong absorption capacity toward visible light. Thus, the FHSB-GQDs produce strong fluorescence emission under visible light excitation.

To evaluate the fluorescence behavior of the FHSB-GQDs, their fluorescence spectra were measured under excitation at different wavelengths. The three-dimensional fluorescence mapping of the FHSB-GQDs shows a single peak (Fig. 4 A) located at a fluorescence emission wavelength of 550 nm under excitation at 490 nm. This indicates that the FHSB-GQDs can produce a much stronger fluorescence emission under excitation with 490-nm visible light than under ultraviolet light excitation. When the excitation wavelength is shorter than 400 nm, the maximum emission wavelength rapidly increases with the excitation wavelength. However, the maximum emission wavelength tends to stabilize when the excitation wavelength is longer than 400 nm (Fig. 4B and C). The FHSB-GQDs can generate one strong yellow fluorescence emission with a maximum emission wavelength of 550 nm under excitation wavelength longer than 400 nm. By contrast, the peak fluorescence intensity of the FHSB-GQDs increases with increasing excitation wavelength within a very wide spectral range from the ultraviolet to the visible region. To evaluate the luminescence efficiency, the fluorescence quantum yield (FQY) of the FHSB-GQDs was measured under excitation with visible light at 490 nm. An FQY value of 53.0 % was obtained, outperforming the other GQDs (Table s1). This verifies that the FHSB-GQDs exhibit high luminescence efficiency under visible light excitation. To examine their photostability, the peak fluorescence intensities under excitation with 490-nm visible light were measured after an FHSB-GQD aqueous solution was irradiated using an ultraviolet lamp of 100 W for different times. Fig. 4D shows that the fluorescence intensities remain stable when the irradiation time is less than 30 min, indicating good photostability. These excellent fluorescence properties render the FHSB-GQDs suitable as an optical probe for bioanalysis and bioimaging. Due to high energy of ultraviolet light, the ultraviolet light excitation may induce one strong blue fluorescence emission from biological macromolecules such as protein and nucleic acid. The resulting background fluorescence will interfere with the fluorescence measurement of GQD. In addition, the ultraviolet light excitation may also cause the cell damage and interference with normal life activities due to high energy of ultraviolet light. However, the above issues can be well resolved using the excitation of low-energy visible light for the fluorescence measurement. In addition, the use of strong yellow fluorescence can improve the sensitivity of fluorescence detection and clarity of cell imaging.

To understand the effect of the different functional groups on the fluorescence behavior, the fluorescence spectra of GQDs, H-GQDs, HS-GQDs, and FHS-GQSs were measured at different excitation wavelengths. Figure s4A and B shows that the maximum emission wavelength linearly increases with increasing excitation wavelength. The plots of the maximum emission wavelength vs. the excitation wavelength are a diagonal line. In contrast with the fluorescence spectrum of the FHSB-GQDs, no platform appears in the diagonal line of the spectrum of the GQDs, indicating a different fluorescence behavior. When the excitation wavelength exceeds 380 nm, the peak fluorescence intensity rapidly decreases with increasing excitation wavelength. Hence, GQDs exhibit extremely low luminescence efficiency under visible light excitation. Similar to GQDs, the H-GQDs show low luminescence efficiency under visible light excitation (Figure s4C and D). This demonstrates that the introduction of a single histidine can alter the fluorescence behavior. However, the fluorescence behavior of the HS-GQDs is different from that of GQDs and the H-GQDs (Figure s4E and F). A platform appears in the plot of the maximum emission wavelength vs. the excitation wavelength for the HS-GQDs. The peak fluorescence intensity reaches its maximum value under excitation with 490-nm visible light. This result demonstrates that the synergy between histidine and serine considerably enhances the luminous efficiency under visible light excitation. Similar to the HS-GQDs, the FHS-GQDs exhibit fluorescence behavior similar to that of the FHSB-GQDs (Figure s4G and H). However, the introduction of folic acid and B increases the peak fluorescence intensity under excitation with 490-nm visible light. Furthermore, the fluorescence spectra and intensities of the GQDs, H-GQDs, HS-GQDs, FHS-GQDs, and FHSB-GQDs under excitation at 360 nm (F420) and 490 nm (F550) were measured. Figure s5 shows that GQDs produce the smallest F420 and F550 values, confirming that GQDs exhibit very low luminescence efficiency under excitation with ultraviolet and visible light. The F550 value of the H-GQDs is slightly larger than that of the GQDs. This demonstrates that the introduction of histidine can improve luminescence efficiency under visible light excitation. The F420 and F550 values of the HS-GQDs are much higher than those of the H-GQDs, especially the F550 value, which is 24-fold higher than that of the H-GQDs. This result indicates that the introduction of serine can substantially enhance the luminescence efficiency under excitation with ultraviolet and visible light. Similar to the HS-GQDs, the introduction of folic acid and B doping improve the luminescence efficiency. Taken together, these results suggest that the excellent fluorescence behavior of the FHSB-GQDs stems from the synergy between folic acid, histidine, serine, and B.

The pH-Responsiveness of FHSB-GQD was investigated by changing the pH stimulation. Fig. 5 A shows the FHSB-GQD aqueous solution can produce strong fluorescence emission under 490 nm visible light excitation. The highest intensity appears in acidic condition of pH 3, whereas the lowest fluorescence intensity occurs in alkaline condition of pH 9. This is because the degrees of dissociation and protonation in functional groups of FHSB-GQD will be changed with the variation of pH stimulation. This will affect the fluorescent properties of FHSB-GQD and leads to a reduced fluorescence intensity. Further, Fig. 5B indicates the fluorescent intensity linearly reduces with the increase of pH from 4 to 9. The linear regression equation is F550 = −724,359 × pH + 8 × 106, R2 = 0.98, indicating a highly sensitive pH-responsiveness. To test the stability of fluorescent properties, the peak fluorescent intensities of FHSB-GQD aqueous solution at pH 4 and pH 9 were measured by the repeated regulation of pH stimulation. Fig. 5C shows that the fluorescence intensity remains almost unchanged during the repeated regulation of pH stimulation. After many times circulations, the fluorescence intensity always remain in a stable region while the pH was fixed at 4 or 9. The result demonstrates the FHSB-GQD not only can offer a sensitive pH-responsiveness, but also maintain one steady fluorescent property during the repeated regulation of pH stimulation. This excellent behavior further broadens the prospects of FHSB-GQD in monitoring the pH variation. To test the selectivity of pH detection, the peak fluorescence intensity of FHSB-GQD was investigated at pH 7 in the presence of interfere species, which may exist in human sweat. It can be seen from Fig. 5D that the presence of 0.1 mg mL−1 serine (Ser), glucose, glycine (Gly), threonine (Thr), histidine (His), ascorbic acid (AA), lactic acid (LA), Ca2+, K+, NH4 +, Na+ and Mg2+ don't obviously influence on the peak fluorescence intensity, indicating a good selectivity. This is because these species don't produce a significant fluorescence emission under the excitation of 490 nm visible light. In addition, these species also cannot strongly combine with the FHSB-GQD and leads to an obvious change in the fluorescence intensity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Construction principle, mechanical property, ultraviolet protection ability, and antibacterial activity of the FHSB-GQD–PVA–PG film
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 6 A shows the construction principle of the FHSB-GQD–PVA–PG film. As PVA is a typical linear polymer, a PVA film exhibits poor mechanical property owing to the lack of a strong interaction force and reliable crosslinking between its molecular chains. To improve the mechanical property, another linear polymer rich in hydroxyl groups, PG, was introduced into PVA. Owing to its special structure, PG strongly interacts with the PVA chains via hydrogen bonds. During repeated freeze–thaw treatment, the PVA and PG chains approach each other to finally produce one molecular network connected via hydrogen bonds, thereby improving the mechanical property. The introduction of the FHSB-GQDs was expected to further improve the mechanical property owing to the presence of many hydroxyl groups at the edges of the graphene sheets in the FHSB-GQDs, which can bind with PVA and PE chains via hydrogen bonds. This binding force enhances the tensile strength and toughness of the FHSB-GQD–PVA–PG film.

Fig. 6B shows the transmittance vs. the wavelength plot for the PVA-PG and FHSB-GQD–PVA–PG films. Both films can absorb ultraviolet light; however, the absorption capacity of the FHSB-GQD–PVA–PG film is much higher than that of the PVA-PG film. In addition, the FHSB-GQD–PVA–PG film shows a certain ability to absorb visible light shorter than 500 nm, which can be attributed to the FHSB-GQDs. However, the FHSB-GQD–PVA–PG film hardly absorbs visible light longer than 500 nm. Thus, the FHSB-GQD–PVA–PG film still maintains good transparency.

Fig. 6C shows the optical photographs of the FHSB-GQD–PVA–PG film in various deformation states under irradiation with a laser pointer of 490 nm. No obvious destruction of the FHSB-GQD–PVA–PG film was observed upon bending, rolling, twisting, and folding, confirming that it can resist deformations caused by external forces. Thus, the FHSB-GQD–PVA–PG film is suitable for fabricating wearable pH sensors. The stress–strain curve of the FHSB-GQD–PVA–PG film is shown in Fig. 6D. At the breakpoint, the strain reaches 644 % with a 0.15-KPa stress force, demonstrating the good mechanical property of the FHSB-GQD–PVA–PG film.

Fig. 6E shows the ultraviolet–visible absorption spectrum of the FHSB-GQD–PVA–PG film. The presence of a wide and strong absorption peak at 283 nm indicates good ultraviolet protection ability, which can be ascribed to the fact that the graphene sheets and aromatic rings in the FHSB-GQDs can strongly absorb ultraviolet light. Unlike common GQDs, the FHSB-GQDs can also absorb high-energy blue light, which is beneficial for protecting human skin from light damage.

Fig. 6F–I shows the optical photographs of the PVA-PG and FHSB-GQD–PVA–PG films after incubation in Escherichia coli and Staphylococcus aureus for 48 h. As shown in Fig. 6F and G, numerous bacteria exist on the PVA-PG film, which demonstrates that the pure PVA-PG film does not exhibit good antibacterial ability. By contrast, no bacteria appear in the FHSB-GQD–PVA–PG film, with the antibacterial rates for Escherichia coli and Staphylococcus aureus being higher than 99 %. This can be attributed to the introduction of the FHSB-GQDs, whose protonated form in water interacts strongly with the hydrophilic groups on the surface of bacteria. Moreover, the positive electric field from the protonated FHSB-GQDs disrupts the normal life activity of bacteria, restricting their growth and reproduction and improving the antibacterial performance.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence behavior of the FHSB-GQD–PVA–PG film
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescence behavior of the FHSB-GQD–PVA–PG film was examined. A highly concentrated center appears under 490-nm excitation and at 542.6-nm emission in the three-dimensional fluorescence mapping (Fig. 7 A). Compared with the FHSB-GQDs, the emissive center of the FHSB-GQD–PVA–PG film was blueshifted from 550 nm. This is related to the fluorescence mechanism of the FHSB-GQDs. In an aqueous solution, the free movement of the FHSB-GQDs causes their aggregation over time. This attenuates the surface-state emission, indicating low-energy and long-wavelength fluorescence. Conversely, the abundant hydrogen bonding between the FHSB-GQDs and PVA and PG chains results in a steady surface-state contribution indicative of high-energy and short-wavelength emission [30]. Similar to the fluorescence behavior of the FHSB-GQDs, the FHSB-GQD–PVA–PG film exhibit an excitation-dependent behavior (Fig. 7B). The peak fluorescent intensity gradually increases with increasing excitation wavelength from 360 to 490 nm. However, no redshift can be observed in Fig. 7B when the excitation wavelength is higher than 400 nm. This result is supported by a CIE coordinate analysis (Fig. 7C), which reveals that the emitting color remains as a strong yellow fluorescence in a wide excitation wavelength range. Furthermore, Fig. 7D shows that the FHSB-GQD–PVA–PG film can produce a strong yellow fluorescence under excitation with a 490 nm laser. These results demonstrate that the FHSB-GQD–PVA–PG film exhibits better fluorescence stability than single FHSB-GQDs. The FHSB-GQD–PVA–PG film produces a higher FQY (62.5 %) than the FHSB-GQD aqueous solution (53 %). This result confirms that the introduction of PVA and PG not only provides a loading substrate for the FHSB-GQDs but also ensures a highly stable fluorescence emission, which can be attributed to the formation of strong hydrogen bonds between the FHSB-GQDs and PVA and PG chains. To confirm this assumption, the FTIR spectra of the PVA-PG and FHSB-GQD–PVA–PG films were measured. As shown in Figure s6, the introduction of the FHSB-GQDs causes a blueshift in the IR absorption peaks owing to the stretching vibration and bending vibration of the O–H bonds of the PVA-PG film from 3441 to 3417 cm−1 and from 1445 to 1418 cm−1, respectively, which demonstrates the formation of new hydrogen bonds between the FHSB-GQDs and PVA and PG chains. This relieves the fluorescence quenching caused by the aggregation of the graphene sheets in the FHSB-GQDs, resulting in an ideal fluorescent behavior of the FHSB-GQD–PVA–PG film.

The pH responsiveness of the FHSB-GQD–PVA–PG film was also investigated. Fig. 7E shows the fluorescence spectra at different pH values. Compared with the fluorescence spectrum of an FHSB-GQD aqueous solution, the introduction of PVA and PG leads to a shape change in the fluorescence spectrum, which is ascribable to the interaction between the FHSB-GQDs and PVA and PG chains. Furthermore, Fig. 7F shows the plot of the peak fluorescence intensity vs. pH. The peak fluorescence intensity linearly decreases with increasing pH within pH 4–9 according to the linear regression equation Fp = −173,700 × pH + 3.1 × 106 (R2 = 0.998). This linear relationship is superior to that of the FHSB-GQD aqueous solution in Fig. 5B because the combination with the PVA and PG restricts the free movement and leads to improved fluorescence stability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application in detection of skin pH
ce:section.ce:para: A wearable pH sensing system based on FHSB-GQD-PVA-PG film was designed and constructed to monitor pH variation in sweat during the motions (Fig. 8 A). In the design, the FHSB-GQD-PVA-PG film was faced and fixed at runner's arm. The sweat produced by the movement was immersed in the film and may cause the pH variation of FHSB-GQD-PVA-PG film. This will lead to a change in the fluorescence signal. At different time points, remove the film and measure its fluorescence spectrum under the excitation of 490 nm visible light on the fluorescence spectrophotometer. Fig. 8B shows after the operation for 40 min the ratio of relative intensity (F/F0) reaches to 1.17, indicating that a significant variation appears in this running.

One wearable intelligent pH sensing system was designed and used for monitoring pH variation in sweat (shown in Fig. 8C). The sensing system includes one laser pointer, one smartphone and one FHSB-GQD-PVA-PG film. The laser pointer can produce 490 nm blue light. It was used as one light source of excitation light for producing fluorescence signal. By the image recognition software, the smartphone was collected fluorescence signal on the FHSB-GQD-PVA-PG film under 490 nm laser irradiation. The FHSB-GQD-PVA-PG film was used as a steady optical probe for producing fluorescence response towards pH variation in sweat. The investigation demonstrated that the results obtained by wearable intelligent pH sensing system are highly consistent with those obtained by using fluorescence photometer. This verifies that wearable intelligent pH sensing system offers a high reliability.

Functionalized FHSB-GQDs were designed and synthesized via thermal treatment. The introduction of histidine, serine, ethylenediamine, and B atoms results in strong yellow fluorescence emission under excitation with 490-nm visible light and sensitive pH responsiveness, which are further improved by introducing PVA and PG. The resulting FHSB-GQD–PVA–PG film exhibits excellent transparency, mechanical flexibility, ultraviolet protection ability, and antibacterial and fluorescence behavior. The luminescent film was successfully applied for the detection of pH changes in human sweat. This study provides a new platform for the design and construction of wearable sensing systems for application in health monitoring, facial masks, and medical dressings.
Xie Qingqing: Investigation, Methodology. Li Ruiyi: Investigation, Validation, Writing – original draft. Li Zaijun: Conceptualization, Investigation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors acknowledge the financial support from The National Key Research and Development Program of China (No.2021YFA0910200) and National Natural Science Foundation of China (No.21576115). We also would like to acknowledge the work of the Central Laboratory, School of Chemical and Material Engineering, Jiangnan University.
